figures.gloss: The supreme sky-creator of the Akan, maker of all things, who shaped the world and then drew back beyond the firmament — too high and too holy for daily worship.
figures.facet: The signature African shard: a high-god who is not dead but RECEDED. A myth tells that an old woman pounding fufu kept striking the sky with her pestle, so Nyame withdrew upward in irritation — divinity made remote not by war but by human nearness.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from Rattray's early-20th-c. Ashanti ethnography and oral Anansesem; the pestle myth is widely attested across Akan and neighbouring peoples.

figures.gloss: The 'Nyame-who-alone-is-great' — the supreme deity under his aspect of shining, solitary sovereignty, often associated with the sun and the title Kwame (Saturday, his day).
figures.facet: Not a separate god but a praise-name: the Akan name the same creator differently for each of his powers, so the one mirror is glimpsed through many faces without ever being divided.

figures.gloss: The old-woman Earth, mother of the dead and giver of crops, second only to Nyame. No farming or grave-digging may begin without her leave, and Thursday (Yaa, her day) is kept sacred from the hoe.
figures.facet: She has no temple and no priesthood — the Akan say the Earth needs no shrine because she IS the shrine. Worship that requires no building: reverence woven directly into the working week.
figures.provenance: Asase Yaa (Ashanti) / Asase Efua (Fante) — the day-name varies by sub-group; rendered here in the dominant Ashanti form.

figures.gloss: The spider who outwits the great: by capturing the python, the hornets and the leopard he bought all the world's stories from Nyame, so that tales are called Anansesem — 'Spider-words' — to this day.
figures.facet: The trickster as KEEPER of narrative itself: not the bringer of fire but the buyer of stories, who turns cunning into the founding deed of culture. The web is the world's first archive.
figures.provenance: Anansesem oral tradition; the 'buying of the stories' tale recorded by Rattray and ubiquitous across Akan and the diaspora (Anansi/Aunt Nancy in the Caribbean and the American South).

figures.gloss: Greatest of the abosom (lesser gods), spirit of the Tano River and lord of war and thunder, eldest son of Nyame contending with his brother Bia for precedence over the world.
figures.facet: A river that is also a thunder-god: in Akan thought the abosom are children of Nyame inhabiting waters and stones, so the storm is not above the land but rises from its very rivers.

figures.gloss: The children of Nyame — the lesser deities of river, lake, sea and tree who manage the world the withdrawn creator left behind, receiving the worship he himself does not.
figures.facet: The structural answer to a remote high-god: where Nyame recedes, the many abosom fill the daily distance — one divinity dispersed downward into the rivers so it stays within reach.

figures.gloss: The honoured dead who dwell in Asamando, the ancestor-land beneath Asase Yaa's keeping, libated and consulted as the living elders of the lineage who guard its continuance.
figures.facet: The dead are not gone but promoted: an Akan does not pray past the ancestors to reach the divine — the lineage's own forebears are the nearest sacred power, closer than any god.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Reincarnated ancestral presences who actively return to the living to enforce lineage law and morality.

Two supreme makers who withdraw upward into the sky after creation — the Australian All-Father and the Akan high god — structurally kin as deus otiosus, by wholly unrelated peoples; never a cognate, only a recurring human answer to the absent maker.
Both a creator who finishes the world and steps back from daily dealing with humankind; a loose withdrawn-maker parallel across wholly separate traditions, flagged.
Same Akan deity under two praise-names; Nyankopon = Nyame-koro-pon, 'the one great Nyame.' Cognate identity within a single language.
The Akan sky/earth couple — withdrawn Sky-father above, working Earth-mother below; the lived world hangs between them.
Within Akan myth: Anansi buys all stories from Nyame, binding the world's narrative to the creator he serves and outwits.
Tano is named son of Nyame; the abosom are the creator's children, an internal Akan genealogy.
The abosom ARE the children of Nyame — the structural device by which a withdrawn creator stays present through many lesser hands.
Tano is the greatest of the abosom; within Akan religion the chief river-god heads the lesser gods.
The ancestors dwell in the earth's keeping; Asase Yaa is mother of the dead as well as of crops.
Two sky-fathers across unrelated families — but where Dyeus stays the active head of his pantheon, Nyame WITHDRAWS. The convergence on sky-sovereignty makes the divergence (deus otiosus) legible.
Sky-father over earth-mother in both, but Ranginui is separated by force while Nyame recedes of his own holiness — two routes to the same remote sky.
Both are supreme sky-powers worshipped less directly than the nearer gods/ancestors — high heaven held in awe at a distance.
An/Anu, the Mesopotamian sky-head, likewise recedes into remote authority while younger gods (Enlil, Marduk) run the world — a parallel deus-otiosus drift.
Earth personified as primal mother and ground of the dead; both are reverenced as the body of the world itself.
Earth-mother beneath a withdrawn/separated sky-father, keeper of crops and of the dead — the world-parents pattern across unrelated families.
Old-woman Earth who both bears and devours, mother of crops and grave of the dead.
Two West African tricksters, neighbours but unrelated in language-family — Esu the Yoruba crossroads-gate, Anansi the Akan spider who buys the stories. Functional kin, not cognates.
The cunning shapeshifter who wins by wit against stronger powers — trickster as destabiliser of the great.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who wrest boons from the gods by guile; Maui fishes up islands, Anansi buys the stories.
The cunning benefactor who takes from heaven and gives to humankind — Prometheus fire, Anansi the tales.
Trickster as patron of speech, story and clever exchange — both make cunning into a civilising art.
West African thunder/war powers in neighbouring but distinct traditions — Sango the Yoruba storm-king, Tano the Akan river-thunder. Structural kin, never cognate.
Storm-and-war power who is the people's nearest protective god while the sky-father stays remote.
A reach: both name the many sacred powers as faces of a single diffused divinity — the one-through-many. Speculative, no contact.
Both supreme, unmade, beyond image — but the parallel is interpretive; Akan Nyame recedes where YHWH demands covenant. Flagged as a stretch.
Both centre the honoured dead as a living sacred power, but Osiris is a dying-rising god where the nsamanfo are simply ancestors promoted — a loose structural echo.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Earth mothers intimately tied to soil fertility who demand strict moral and ritual observance to prevent disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly localized nature spirits who demand specific transactional offerings to grant favors or withhold disease.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly structured hierarchy of divine delegates who manage the specific departments of creation on behalf of a withdrawn, supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Venerated ancestral spirits who hold punitive and protective power over their living descendants, demanding regular propitiation.
GOLD SEAM: Traditions where the revered dead do not depart for an ethereal afterlife, but remain physically present to actively adjudicate the affairs of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The un-anthropomorphized, literal earth-as-mother who receives the dead and dictates agricultural and pastoral fertility.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A theological system where the unresolved grievances of the ancestors actively block the prosperity and spiritual progress of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are physically weak animal tricksters who rely on deceit and cleverness to humiliate larger, stronger adversaries, often overreaching in the process.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent unseen but profoundly localized spirit societies that seamlessly co-occupy the physical geography alongside the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Venerated ancestors who hold punitive and protective power over their direct living descendants.
GOLD SEAM: The forebears whose continued existence depends on ritual remembrance, and who act as strict moral guardians of their descendants' behavior.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Earth revered not merely as a resource, but as a sacred, living entity that dictates ethical human behavior.
GOLD SEAM: The conviction that the ancestors remain active participants in the family unit, requiring ongoing ritual sustenance and offering moral grounding to the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The maternal figure strictly associated with the earth, localized cultivation, and the intimate life-giving forces of the ground.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign earth-mothers inextricably bound to the physical landscape and fertility of their people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Groups of revered ancestors actively involved in guiding, protecting, and occasionally disciplining their living descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A neighboring supreme sky-god parallel, functionally similar in governing rain and the ultimate life force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Active, localized deities inhabiting natural features, mediating between humans and a supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly localized, intermediary deities tied to specific lineages and geographical features, rather than universal pantheonic domains.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancestors conceptualized not as distant spirits, but as an active, approaching force vital to the survival of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the earth itself as a deeply immanent, sacred feminine principle that demands ethical stewardship and reciprocity from humans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Emanated, operative deities tasked with doing the actual worldly governance for a creator who has entirely withdrawn from the mundane sphere.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the marginalized utilizing guile, humor, and trickery to outwit a physically superior oppressor, becoming folk heroes of survival.

GOLD SEAM: two West African high-gods who create then RECEDE, leaving no daily cult while nearer powers (alusi / abosom) receive worship — the deus-otiosus pattern across unrelated Niger-Congo branches (Igbo and Akan). Structural kin, not cognates.

GOLD SEAM: two West African earth-mothers, second only to the withdrawn sky-creator, who are the source of moral law and the keepers of the dead — Igbo Ala and Akan Asase Yaa, functional kin across branches.
GOLD SEAM: two West African ancestor-cults where the honoured dead are the nearest sacred power, closer than the withdrawn creator — Igbo ndichie and Akan nsamanfo, functional kin across branches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Active ancestral spirits who demand regular libations and fiercely regulate the morality of the living.
Two African high-gods who CREATE then RECEDE, leaving no daily cult — the deus-otiosus pattern shared across unrelated Niger-Congo branches (Akan and Bantu), structural kin not cognates.
The many lesser water-spirits who manage the world a withdrawn creator left behind — bisimbi and abosom are the same answer to a remote high-god, in neighbouring but distinct Niger-Congo branches.
Two African ancestor-cults where the honoured dead are the nearest sacred power, closer than the withdrawn creator — Kongo bakulu and Akan nsamanfo, functional kin across branches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spirits that anchor divine authority into localized natural topography, requiring strict territorial propitiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deeply venerated ancestral spirits whose accrued power directly dictates the earthly fortune and protection of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shape-shifting spider trickster spirit who simultaneously builds and complicates the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancestors functioning as localized guardians who mediate all moral boundaries and ecological access.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions elevate the active, propitiated dead (ancestors) to equal ritual importance alongside major deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are multitudes of operative, intermediate spirits handling specific cosmic and earthly functions for a remote creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Revered ancestral spirits who demand continuous propitiation and can aggressively intervene in their descendants' lives.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are physically weak but intensely intelligent tricksters who triumph over larger, stronger adversaries through rapid manipulation and sociopathy.
GOLD SEAM: Both serve as venerated, spiritually elevated ancestors who actively intercede in the material world to guide their descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the Earth not as dead matter to be exploited, but as a living entity demanding respect and maintaining ecological balance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Localized, ever-present ancestors who demand literal sustenance (meat/libations) and fiercely police the moral boundaries of their descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hyper-local tutelary spirits bound to natural features (trees, stones) who act as moral enforcers for the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ethereal ancestors who maintain acute interest in the moral and property-related affairs of their living descendants.

Two African trickster-shapers in small bodies — the Akan spider and the |Xam mantis — both creator-adjacent culture-heroes who carry the world's order in an insect or arachnid form. Functional kin across unrelated families, never cognate.

Two southern/west African supreme makers who create and recede — but Nyame withdraws upward into the sky while Kaang dissolves into the eland and the hunt; the African deus-otiosus diverging into heaven versus into the game.

A faint regional touch: both gather fertility and the giving of life into a single sacred locus tied to the land, but Asase Yaa is the earth herself and the eland an animal sacrament — different bodies of the same dependence. Flagged.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme creator who established the cosmos but is now utterly withdrawn, delegating active governance to a pantheon of lesser, interactive spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An earth-bound female divinity deeply tied to strict agricultural taboos and the sanctity of the un-tilled soil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active dead who constantly oversee the living, ensuring that strict customary laws and tribal traditions are upheld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spiritual entities that blend ancestral human history with raw natural forces, acting as delegates for a supreme creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Elevated ancestral spirits whose ongoing propitiation is strictly required for the community's fortune and health.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deeply localized, living earth-mother whose profoundly biological rhythms and taboos dictate ritual reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Functionally active ancestral guardians who demand reciprocal offerings to maintain the protective boundary around the family.
GOLD SEAM: Both function as unseen, discarnate ancestors who provide active guidance, protection, and wisdom to the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, venerated shades of the ancestors who are directly invoked to guide the living in spiritual and daily matters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancestors functioning as the true guardians of communal morality and the immediate recipients of petition.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, omnipotent sky creator who establishes the moral order and is universally acknowledged as the absolute source of life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and theological continuity from the Akan supreme sky god to the Surinamese Winti creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Akan earth goddess retained and adapted as the head of the Surinamese terrestrial pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Akan concept of venerated, active ancestors mapping directly onto the Afro-Surinamese Kabra.

Two African supreme beings who recede from daily worship — but Nyame is a sky-creator who withdraws upward, while Unkulunkulu recedes DOWNWARD into the ancestral reed-bed. The shared deus-otiosus drift makes the African divergence (heaven vs. lineage) legible.

Two African cults of the honoured dead — the Akan nsamanfo and the Nguni amadlozi — in which one's own forebears, not a remote god, are the nearest sacred power. Functional kin across unrelated families, never cognate.

The rebellion oath's grave-dirt-and-blood draught invokes the binding witness of the dead in the manner of Gold Coast (Akan) oath practice carried by Coromantee captives (Vincent Brown 2020); the further claim that the word 'obeah' itself is Akan (obayifo) is contested — tier-flagged, not asserted.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Akan peoples of Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire — Asante (Ashanti), Fante, Akuapem, and kin — organized around a withdrawn sky-creator, Nyame, his delegated lesser gods (abosom), the Earth Asase Yaa, and above all the lineage ancestors (nsamanfo). It remains a living tradition, interwoven since the nineteenth century with Christianity.
dossier.cosmos: Nyame made the world and receded upward — the widely told tale blames an old woman's fufu pestle striking the sky — leaving daily governance to the abosom, his children seated in rivers, lakes, and shrines. The human person is composite: kra (life-soul from Nyame), sunsum (personality-spirit), mogya (mother's blood, fixing the matriclan), and ntoro (the father's line). One who lived well and receives proper funeral rites becomes an ancestor in Asamando, retaining real authority over the living lineage.
dossier.practice: Libation and offering are the core acts — to ancestors at blackened stools during the recurring Adae rites, and to abosom at shrines served by trained priests (akomfo) who speak for the god in possession trance. Thursday is traditionally kept from farm work as Asase Yaa's day, and funerals are the largest social-religious events, since they are what make ancestors. Among the Asante, the Golden Stool, held to house the nation's soul (sunsum), fuses religion with kingship.
dossier.canon: Oral: libation prayers, proverbs, drum poetry, and the Anansesem story corpus; the earliest systematic documentation is R. S. Rattray's Asante ethnography of the 1920s.
dossier.reflects: The sacred routed through lineage — a high god held in awe at a distance while daily religious traffic runs to ancestors and delegated powers close at hand.
sources: R. S. Rattray, 'Religion and Art in Ashanti' (1927)
J. B. Danquah, 'The Akan Doctrine of God' (1944)
Kofi Asare Opoku, 'West African Traditional Religion' (1978)
